coredata.dc:title: Lignin-derived hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets decorated with biomass carbon quantum dots for efficient oxygen reduction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Zihao
Han, Ying
Wang, Xing
Sun, Guangwei
Li, Yao
coredata.dc:description: Excavating and developing efficient and economical non-metallic nanomaterial catalysts for oxygen reduction reactions (ORR), which are vital for fuel cells, but is still challenging. Here, we synthesize a nitrogen-doped hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets decorated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. The hierarchically porosity heteroatom-doped flower-like carbon nanosheets are first synthesized using MgO as hard template, KOH/NaOH as activator, lignin/urea as carbon/heteroatom source, and then lignin-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fabricated via microwave hydrothermal are compounded with carbon nanosheets after a one-step hydrothermal treatment. Benefitting from the interconnection of its 3D graphite shell network, abundant active sites, high specific surface area, and multi-scale pore structure, the n-HPFN@CQDs exhibits favorable ORR activity and stability in 0.1 mol KOH media. Compared with commercial Pt/C, the assembled Zn-air batteries using n-HPFN@CQDs as electrode catalysts exhibit comparable discharge performance. This work synthesized a novel and green biomass-based carbon composite materials achieving high-performance for energy conversion applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Flower-like nanosheets
Carbon quantum dots
Oxygen reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of aminated lignin-based carbon quantum dots (n-CQDs)
Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped activated carbon (n-AC)
Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped porous flower-like carbon nanosheets (n-PFN)
Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets (n-HPFN)
Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets decorated with n-CQDs (n-HPFN@CQDs)
The component and properties of AL

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908014 ), Department of Education of Liaoning Province Project (No. J2020038 ), Liaoning University Innovation Talent Program in 2020, the Special Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020T130464 ) and the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University (No. 2021KF01 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Modification mechanism of lignin with ethylene diamine (a); Side-chain and aromatic region in the 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of the lignin (b).

SEM images of Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 template (a), n-AC (b), n-HPFN@CQDs (c, d); TEM images of Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 template (e), n-CQDs (f) and n-HPFN@CQDs (g, h); Elemental mapping images of n-HPFN@CQDs (i); Schematic Illustration of the Synthesis Process of n-HPFN@CQDs (j).
N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of all samples (a) and pore size distribution of all samples (b).
XRD patterns of all samples (c); Raman analyses of all samples (d).
XPS survey spectra of all samples (a); n-AL, n-CQDs and n-HPFN@CQDs in different types of nitrogen (b); XPS N 1s spectrum (c) and C 1s spectrum (d) of n-HPFN@CQDs.

CV curves in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH (a), ORR LSV curves of the different catalysts in 0.1 M KOH (b), LSV curves of n-HPFN@CQDs with a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1 at different rotating speeds ranging from 400 to 2025 rpm (c), Koutecky-Levich plots of n-HPFN@CQDs (d), Tafel slope curves of n-HPFN@CQDs (e), and Chronoamperometric response for n-HPFN@CQDs and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution (f).

Schematic diagram of liquid Zn-air batteries (a). Open circuit voltage of n-HPFN@CQDs and Pt/C (b), Polarization curves and power density of the Zn-air batteries based on n-HPFN@CQDs and Pt/C (c), Constant current discharge curves of the Zn-air batteries based on n-HPFN@CQDs and Pt/C at different current densities (d), and the two HPFN@CQDs Zn-air batteries based on n-HPFN@CQDs connected in series powered the LED device.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Specific surface area estimated by the BET method

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Total pore volume estimated by BJH formula.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average pore diameter estimated by BJH formula.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of various carbon-based electrocatalysts for ORR.

head.ce:title: Lignin-derived hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets decorated with biomass carbon quantum dots for efficient oxygen reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Key Lab of Lignocellulose Chemistry and BioMaterials, Liaoning Collaborative Innovation Center for Lignocellulosic Biorefinery, College of Light Industry and Chemical Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Key Lab of Lignocellulose Chemistry and BioMaterials, Liaoning Collaborative Innovation Center for Lignocellulosic Biorefinery, College of Light Industry and Chemical Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Key Lab of Lignocellulose Chemistry and BioMaterials, Liaoning Collaborative Innovation Center for Lignocellulosic Biorefinery, College of Light Industry and Chemical Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excavating and developing efficient and economical non-metallic nanomaterial catalysts for oxygen reduction reactions (ORR), which are vital for fuel cells, but is still challenging. Here, we synthesize a nitrogen-doped hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets decorated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. The hierarchically porosity heteroatom-doped flower-like carbon nanosheets are first synthesized using MgO as hard template, KOH/NaOH as activator, lignin/urea as carbon/heteroatom source, and then lignin-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fabricated via microwave hydrothermal are compounded with carbon nanosheets after a one-step hydrothermal treatment. Benefitting from the interconnection of its 3D graphite shell network, abundant active sites, high specific surface area, and multi-scale pore structure, the n-HPFN@CQDs exhibits favorable ORR activity and stability in 0.1 mol KOH media. Compared with commercial Pt/C, the assembled Zn-air batteries using n-HPFN@CQDs as electrode catalysts exhibit comparable discharge performance. This work synthesized a novel and green biomass-based carbon composite materials achieving high-performance for energy conversion applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article. All data included in this study are available upon request by contact with the corresponding author.

ce:section.ce:para: The over-utilization of fossil resources as well as the accompanying greenhouse effect and environmental degradation have been attracting widespread research interest in developing environmentally friendly, sustainable electrical energy storage and conversion technologies [1,2]. Through electrocatalytic reaction, the chemical energy of raw materials is transformed into electric energy for storage and reuse, which has the characteristics of high energy density, safety, convenience, and a wide range of available materials. Zn-air batteries are recognized as one of the most promising energy technologies attributed to their high specific energy, abundant resources, safety and being pollution-free, promising to be an environmentally friendly, efficient, and sustainable new energy resource technology [3]. However, the slow kinetics of ORR during negative charge seriously hinders the expected performance of Zn-air batteries [4]. Thus, efficient ORR electrocatalysts are required to overcome the electrochemical reaction energy barriers in electrochemical reactions and increase the reaction rate [5–7]. Although noble metals (Pt) are the most active and widely used electrocatalysts for ORR [8,9], their low reserves, high prices and poor durability significantly limit their application in large-scale energy devices [10]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop high catalytic activity ORR non-precious metal electrocatalysts and non-metal electrocatalysts [11–14].

Compared with metal-based nanomaterials, carbon-based catalysts offer superior durability, outstanding resistance to toxicity, and excellent catalytic selectivity. As an idea carbon source, biomass has the advantages of abundant reserves, inexpensive, and diversity of composition and structure. The conversion of biomass into carbon nanomaterials is one of the most attractive and effective ways to obtain electrocatalysts from economical, environmentally friendly, abundant, sustainable, and renewable resources in recent years [15–20]. Carbon materials derived from biomass resources exhibit great potential in catalytic reactions such as batteries, supercapacitors, ORR [21], OER [22], and CO2 capture [23]. Lignin, a by-product of the paper and pulping industries with abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups and high carbon content (55–60%), is the third most abundant natural polymer after cellulose and chitosan, which is an excellent carbon precursor [24]. However, the major application of lignin is the combustion of heat energy with high CO2 emission. Over the past few years, lignin-derived carbon fibers, lignin-based activated carbon and other nano carbon materials have attracted increasing attention from researchers [25,26]. Lignin as a carbon precursor to prepare highly active carbon-based electrocatalysts not only provides a low-cost and renewable source of electrocatalysts, but also realizes a high-value utilization of lignin.

In recent years, three-dimensional flower-like carbon nanosheets have attracted enormous attention in the field of electrocatalysis owing to its high specific surface area ultra-thin thickness and interconnected pore network. However, the electrochemical applications of three-dimensional flower-like carbon nanosheets are limited by their inherent poor conductivity and less active sites. Chemical activation can significantly increase the density of active sites of carbon materials as the activation process creates structural defect sites such as edges, corners, vacancies, and grain boundaries, while the porous structures resulting from chemical activation can significantly increase the specific surface area, thus increasing the accessibility of active sites. Moreover, carbon quantum dots (CQD) can also be applied as active sites to enhance the electrocatalytic ability of carbon nanosheets. CQDs are a carbon-based 0-dimensional material with excellent electrical conductivity, tunable band gap, and extraordinary optoelectronic properties owing to their significant quantum-bound, highly exposed surface and edge effects [27–29], which has led to widespread applications in energy-related fields such as photovoltaic devices, pollutant detection [30], and Zn-air batteries [31]. It was found that hybrid composites based on functionalized CQDs can exhibit the collective properties of individual CQDs, especially as catalysts or supports toward ORR applications [32]. For instance, N-doped CQDs exhibits remarkable ORR performance in alkaline solutions for its unique defect structure [33], which is generally considered as the main catalytically active site of carbon materials. In addition, CQDs can be used as a support matrix for Pt nanoparticles that exhibit higher electrocatalytic activity in ORR [34]. However, as far as we know, the catalytic performance of hierarchical porosity heteroatom-doped flower-like carbon nanosheets loaded with CQDs applied to ORR has not been reported.

In the present work, we have assembled nitrogen-doped 3D hierarchically porous flower-like carbon nanosheets with lignin as carbon precursor, urea as nitrogen source, nano MgO as template and KOH/NaOH as activator. Then, nitrogen-doped CQDs were prepared from aminated lignin by microwave hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 1 h. Finally, we successfully assemble nitrogen-doped CQDs in situ decorated onto nitrogen-doped 3D hierarchically porous flower-like carbon nanosheets to form unique nanohybrids by a simple one-step microwave hydrothermal technique. The n-HPNC@CQD shows remarkably enhanced electrocatalytic activity for ORR compared to that of original n-HPNC. This composite with unique morphology and structure has a high specific surface area with abundant layered interconnected pore structure, avoiding the agglomeration of CQDs. The n-HPNC@CQDs for Zn-air batteries also exhibits extraordinary discharge performance comparable to Pt/C catalysts.

Typically, aminated lignin is synthesized by Mannich reaction from alkaline lignin, ethylenediamine and formaldehyde. First, dissolve 5 g of alkaline lignin in 25 ml of 1 M aqueous sodium hydroxide and stir for 30 min at room temperature to ensure complete dissolution of the lignin. Then, 15 g of ethylenediamine and 20 g of formaldehyde were added dropwise under stirring. The mixture was stirred and reacted at 70 °C for 3 h, then adjusted to pH= 7 with 1 mol/L HCl, filtered, repeatedly washed, and then freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of aminated lignin-based carbon quantum dots (n-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 0.1 g of aminated lignin was dissolved in 10 ml of deionized water and ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min. The mixed solution was put into a microwave hydrocube at 200 °C for 1 h. After cooling, the water was filtered through a 0.22-micron microporous membrane t to disperse the n-CQDs and remove the undispersed carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped activated carbon (n-AC)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, lignin, urea and KOH were dissolved in 30 ml of distilled water at a mass ratio of 1:1:1 and stirred for 6 h to obtain a well-mixed solution, then dried in an oven at 60 °C for one day. The samples were placed in a combustion boat and carbonized in tube furnace. Increasing the carbonization temperature from room temperature at 5 °C min− 1 to 900 °C and maintaining it in a nitrogen atmosphere for 1 h. Finally, the carbonized product was washed by 1 M HCL, the product was collected by centrifugation and washed repeatedly with deionized water to remove the salts.

0.076 g of CTAB, 5.4 g of urea and 3.7 g of MgCl2·H2O were fully dissolved in 60 ml of distilled water to form a clarified solution. The mixture was held at 100 °C for 12 h followed by standing at 95 °C for 12 h. Finally, the solid products are obtained by centrifuged and then freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped porous flower-like carbon nanosheets (n-PFN)
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 0.5 g of lignin, 0.5 g urea, and 1 g KOH were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water with magnetic continuous stirring, respectively. After 0.5 h, the 5 g MgO template was added into the mixture solution and stirred for 6 h, the mixture was dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and then the solid products heated in a tubular furnace at 900 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 under a nitrogenous atmosphere. The obtained black powder was repeatedly washed with 1 M dilute hydrochloric acid solution. Finally, n-PFN was obtained by filtration, repeated washing, and sufficient drying in an oven at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets (n-HPFN)
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.5 g of lignin, 0.5 g urea, 0.5 g NaOH, and 0.5 g KOH were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water with magnetic continuous stirring, respectively. And then the n-HPFN was prepared with the same procedure as n-PFN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped hierarchical porous flower-like carbon nanosheets decorated with n-CQDs (n-HPFN@CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The Synthesis Process of n-HPFN@CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 2j. For preparation of the n-HPFN @CQDs, 5 mg n-CQDs was added into 10 ml deionized water for ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min, and then 10 ml n-GQDs solutions and 50 mg n- HPFN were put into a microwave hydrocube and heated at 220 °C for 1 h. The mixture solutions were cooled down to room temperature, then the n-HPFN @CQDs were freeze-dried. Finally, the n-HPNC@CQDs were transferred to a tube furnace and carbonized at 400 °C for 1 h under a nitrogen atmosphere at a ramp rate of 5 °C min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The component and properties of AL
ce:section.ce:para: The carbohydrate contents of AL are given in Table S1. The AL contained small amounts of sugars, amounting to 0.04% and 0.68%. The main carbohydrate in AL is xylose (0.68%), accounting for 59.1% of the total carbohydrate, indicating that AL may contain more linear hemicellulose (xylan), followed by glucose, accounting for 21.7% of the total carbohydrate, and other carbohydrate content is low.

2D-HSQC is an effective method for in-depth analysis of the lignin structure (
Fig. 1). In the side chain linkage bond region (C/H 50–90/2.5–6.0 ppm) of lignin, the predominantly presented is the most widespread β-O-4 linkage aryl ether bond (A, A' and A′') structure and phenyl coumarin (β-5, C). The presence of methoxy signals in the structural units (-OCH3, δC/δH 54.2/3.73) indicates that the lignin fraction is rich in aromatic groups of syringyl (S) units and guaiacyl (G) units. In the aromatic region (C/H 80–140/5.5–8.0 ppm), as expected, the δC/δH signals of S, G and p-hydroxyphenyl (H) units can be clearly observed. The correlations for C2,6–H2,6 from S units at δC/δH 104.7/6.71 ppm, while the Cα=O type S units was observed at 106.7/7.29 ppm. The G units correlations can be observed at 114.7/6.95 (C5–H5), 119.2/6.80 (C6–H6), and 110.9/6.98 (C2–H2), respectively. The H units exhibited correlations for C2,6-H2,6 at δC/δH 129.4/7.19 ppm. These both indicated that the lignin is typical GSH- type lignin.

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms was carried out to characterize the porosity of different samples. As shown in Fig. 3a, n-AC prepared without Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 template exhibits Type I curve, demonstrating the presence of abundant micropores. The BET and average pore diameter were 1288.76 m2 g−1 and 2.14 nm ( Table 1), respectively, and the pore size distribution analysis also indicated that only micropores existed [35]. The n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs exhibit type IV curve with H4 hysteresis loops, demonstrating the existence of both microporous and mesopores in the materials [36]. The specific surface area of n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs were 1544.08 m2 g−1 with average pore diameter of 4.45 nm, 1962.87 m2 g−1 with average pore diameter of 5.73 nm and 1860.39 m2 g−1 with average pore diameter of 5.46 nm, respectively, and the high surface area could provide plentiful exposed active sites [37]. The rapid increase in nitrogen adsorption for all samples at lower relative pressures indicates the existence of a high amount of micropores in the material, and the micropores originate from the etching of KOH. There is a sharply increased nitrogen adsorption for n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs in the high relative pressure, which is attributed to the accumulation of non-rigid lamellar pores [38]. The results show that abundant mesopores can be obtained on the surface of materials through additionally introduction of Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 as the hard templates compared to n-AC without Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 templates. The mesopores are attributed to the Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 nanosheets after high temperature heat treatment have been transformed into MgO cube particles with nanometer size of 40–80 nm, instead of retaining the original sheet structure, and then the nano sized MgO cube particles in the sample carbon was removed by dilute hydrochloric acid and formed macropores and mesopores in the carbon frame. As shown in Fig. 3b, the pore size distributions of n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs at 1–10 nm displays micropores less than 2 nm and mesopores centered at 4 nm, indicating that n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs have hierarchically porous structure. The surface area and peak strength in mesopore area of n-HPFN and n-HPFN@CQDs increased remarkably in comparison with n-PFN (Fig. 3b and Table 1). The KOH interacts with NaOH to form KOH/NaOH eutectic mixture, and mesopores and micropores can be formed during the activation process, while KOH usually produces micropores [39].

The crystal structure of electrocatalyst was characterized by XRD. In Fig. 4a, all samples exhibit two broad peaks centered at around 24° and 43.5°, which were ascribed to the (002) and (100) reflections, respectively, indicating that the material has a typical amorphous structure with graphitic phase [40]. Compared with n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs, the (002) peak intensity of n-AC is relatively weak, faintly broadened, and shifted to lower diffraction angle, suggesting their more disordered structure. The Raman spectrum of all samples (Fig. 4b) show two distinct bands located at 1355 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to D band (defects and disordered carbon) and G band (ideal graphitic lattices) [41]. In general, the value of the ID/IG ratio is usually used to reflect the degree of graphitization of the carbon material [42]. The ID/IG ratios of the n-AC, n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs are 0.97, 0.98, 1.04, and 1.03, respectively. The ID/IG values of n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs are higher than n-AC, and n-PFN, indicating that there are more defects in n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs. The defects in the carbon framework facilitate the modulation of the local electron distribution of neighboring carbon atoms and promote stronger -OOH adsorption to catalyze ORR [43,44].

FT-IR spectroscopy investigated the chemical composition of all samples. The characteristic peaks of n-AL are basically consistent with AL, indicating that the basic skeletal structure of lignin remains largely intact after amination (Fig. S2). However, there are obvious differences between the two curves, and the absorption bands in the spectra of aminated lignin are substantially enhanced at 1610 cm−1, which can be assigned to the N/H stretching vibration, and the narrow peaks at 634 and 827 cm−1 are the in-plane deformation absorption peaks of amine N-H bonds, respectively [45]. In elemental analysis (Table S2), the nitrogen content in the aminated lignin (11.36%) is significantly higher than that of alkaline lignin (4.78%), consistent with the FT-IR results, demonstrating the successful amination modification of lignin. The spectrum of the n-AC, n-PFN, n-HPFN, and n-HPFN@CQDs displayed only weak absorption peaks associated with O-H (3444 cm−1), CO, CC (1606 cm−1), and C-O (1150 cm−1), implying that the carbonation and activation steps result in the removal of surface functional groups from lignin. The XPS spectrum also show the chemical composition of C, N and O in the n-AL, n-CQDs and n-HPFN@CQDs, with the corresponding content results shown in Table S3. The high-resolution N 1 s spectra ( Fig. 5c) are exhibited the n-HPFN@CQDs can be fitted into three typical nitrogen peaks corresponding to Pyridine-N (398.39 eV), Pyrrolic–N (399.77 eV) and Graphitic–N (401.16 eV), which are also supported by the C–N–C peak in the C 1 s spectrum of n-HPFN@CQDs (Fig. 5d). The N 1 s XPS spectra confirm the successful doping of nitrogen into the carbon framework. Fig. 5b show the nitrogen content of the different samples. The results showed that n-HPFN@CQDs had a high content of pyridine nitrogen (31.48%) and graphite nitrogen (34.30%). As recent research shows, Pyridine N is significantly increasing the ORR activity of the material [46,47], and Graphite N can reduce the intrinsic resistance of the carbon materials and promote the transfer of electrons through the carbon matrix [48,49]. Since high-performance fuel cells require efficient ORR processes, n-HPFN@CQDs with high nitrogen doping would be the ideal catalyst in ORR ( Table 2).

All samples were characterized by SEM and TEM to examine their morphological structure. As shown in Fig. 2f, the n-CQDs are monodisperse with a uniform diameter distribution in the range of 4–10 nm. The overall three-dimensional flower-like spherical structure of the as prepared Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 (Fig. 2a) with a size of about 20–40 µm and the flower-like structure is assembled by ultrathin nanosheets. From the TEM image (Fig. 2e), Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 microspheres are connected by nanosheets with a thickness of 10–20 nm to form flower-like clusters. The Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 templates can generate abundant interconnected macropores, which are essential for the uniform coating of lignin on the sheets. There is almost no morphological difference between n-PFN (Fig. S4a), n-HPFN (Fig. S4b), and n-HPFN@CQDs (Fig. 2c), both exhibit a three-dimensional flower-like structure consisting of corrugated nanosheets, suggesting that the n-PFN, n-HPFN and n-HPFN@CQDs inherited the morphology of Mg5(CO3)2(OH)4. The SEM (Fig. 2d) and TEM (Fig. 2h) shows that the n-HPFN@CQDs with abundant mesopores and macropores, also consistent with the pore size distribution curve (Fig. 3b). As can be seen in Fig. 2g, the thickness of the corrugated nanosheets that comprise the n-HPFN@CQDs is approximately 5 nm. The EDS in Fig. 2i displayed that the N element is uniformly distributed on the carbon nanosheets, confirming the successful doping of the nitrogen.

To evaluate the ORR activity, oxygen-saturated 0.1 mol KOH was used as the electrolyte, CV and LSV were used to evaluate the as-prepared electrocatalysts. ORR activity was first assessed by CV testing. As can be seen in Fig. 6a, the position of the cathodic peaks of n-HPFN@CQDs is more positive than those of the other electrocatalysts, demonstrating that n-HPFN@CQDs had the highest electrocatalytic activity among these electrocatalysts.

As can be seen in Fig. 6b, the half-wave potential of n-HPFN@CQDs (E1/2) is 0.847 V and the limiting current density (JL) is 4.60 mA cm−2, which are superior to those of n-AC (E1/2 = 0.602 V, JL = 2.44 mA cm−2), n-PFN (E1/2 = 0.818 V, JL = 4.16 mA cm−2) and n-HPFN (E1/2 = 0.837 V, JL = 4.38 mA cm−2), as well as outperforming most of the reported carbon-based materials ( Table 3). The tafel slope of n-HPFN@CQDs (Fig. 6e) is smaller (88 mV dec−1) compared to other prepared electrocatalysts, indicating that the n-HPFN@CQDs catalyst has better ORR kinetic behavior. n-HPFN@CQDs and n-HPFN exhibit higher ORR activity than n-PFN, which is attributed to its microporous/mesoporous structure. Micropores facilitate the exposure of abundant active sites, while macro- and mesopores facilitate the rapid mass transfer and enhance the accessibility of the active site. The n-HPFN@CQDs exhibited the highest electrocatalytic activity due to the excellent electron donor/acceptor properties of n-CQDs, the intrinsic structural defects and the N doping that produced more electrocatalytic active sites. Moreover, the n-CQDs and the n-HPFN structure provides high oxygen adsorption energy to promote O2 binding during ORR. The interface and coupling of n-CQDs with n-HPFN facilitate the adsorption of oxygenated species during ORR, thus reducing the overpotential. Furthermore, the value of electron transfer number was determined by the K–L plot (Fig. 6d). The number of electrons transferred per oxygen molecule reduced in the ORR catalyzed by n-HPFN@CQDs is 4, demonstrating that n-HPFN@CQDs catalyzes an oxygen reduction reaction pathway close to 4e-. The durability of n-HPFN@CQDs was measured by long-term chronoamperometric responses (Fig. 6f). Commercial Pt/C catalysts decrease rapidly within the first 2000 s, reducing to only 64.3% of the initial value after 20,000 s. In contrast, the n-HPFN@CQDs was able to maintain 85.9% of its initial current density. It proves that the n-HPFN@CQDs have significantly better long-term stability than Pt/C under alkaline conditions. Moreover, after the addition of 3 M methanol, the current density of n-HPFN@CQDs changed little (Fig. S6), while the current density of Pt/C decreased rapidly.

To further demonstrate the catalytic activity of the prepared n-HPFN@CQDs in a practical energy conversion device, a rechargeable Zn-air batteries was assembled and evaluated with commercial Pt/C and n-HPFN@CQDs as air electrodes, and 6 M KOH and 0.2 M (CH3COO)2Zn as electrolyte, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7b, n-HPFN@CQDs based Zn-air batteries have an open circuit voltage of 1.49 V, which is closed to the open circuit potential of 1.51 V for commercial Pt/C-based Zn-air batteries.

For n-HPFN@CQDs, the peak power density of the battery is up to 133 mW cm−2 at 0.63 V (Fig. 7c), slightly higher than that of commercial Pt/C (131 mW cm−2), indicating a well charge-discharge capability. Moreover, two groups of n-HPFN@CQDs type Zn-air batteries in series can power red light-emitting diodes (LEDs, 3–5 V) at high brightness for a long period of time (Fig. 7e). The constant current density discharge tests of Zn-air batteries assembled by n-HPFN@CQDs and Pt/C were carried out. In Fig. 7d, the voltage of n-HPFN@CQDs at different current densities during constant current discharge is also close to the commercial Pt/C. The performance of n-HPFN@CQDs-based Zn-air batteries is comparable to that of commercial Pt/C.

In summary, the hierarchical porous nitrogen-doped 3D nanosheets from lignin were prepared by hard template method and KOH/NaOH eutectic, and then the n-CQDs in-situ onto lignin-based hierarchical porous nitrogen-doped 3D nanosheets to form n-HPFN@CQDs by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Immobilization of n-CQDs into porous materials can effectively avoid the aggregation of n-CQDs, especially the synergistic effect of n-CQDs and porous materials could provide a superior electrocatalytic performance for composites. The n-HPFN@CQDs are enriched with defective sites and active N dopants, which are the active sites of the electrocatalyst. Moreover, the n-HPFN@CQDs with microporous/ mesoporous/ structures can provide high electrochemically active surface area and can expose enough defect active sites to reduce spatial site resistance and mass transfer resistance. Therefore, the prepared n-HPFN@CQDs showed excellent electrocatalytic activity. When applied as an air-cathode catalyst in Zn-air batteries, the n-HPFN@CQDs possess excellent discharge performance comparable to Pt/C. We anticipate that this work could provide valuable ideas for the development of an efficient, green and cost-effective non-metallic ORR electrocatalyst.

Zihao Ma: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yao Li: Resources. Guangwei Su: Formal analysis. Xing Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation. Ying Han: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908014), Department of Education of Liaoning Province Project (No. J2020038), Liaoning University Innovation Talent Program in 2020, the Special Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020T130464) and the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University (No. 2021KF01).

ce:section.ce:para: The corresponding author consents on behalf of all the authors that this is original work and has permission to be published.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129818.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A

.
